output:
# Research Proposal: Genuine vs. Shortcut Negation Understanding in Instruction-Tuned LLMs

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Negation remains one of the most stubborn failure points for modern NLP architectures. A central reason is distributional: humans express themselves affirmatively far more often than negatively, so negated text is underrepresented in training corpora (Ettinger, 2020). Negation is also *formally diverse*. Beyond explicit lexical/morphological markers ("not", "n't", "no", "cannot"), there is implicit negation — pervasive in clinical text where acronyms denote absence (NAD = "no abnormality detected") and phrases like "normal chest x-ray scan" imply the absence of a finding — and a family of hard compositional cases: negation interacting with quantifiers ("not all" ≠ "none"), gradable adjectives ("not unattractive" does not entail "attractive"; ¬happy ≠ sad), negative polarity items (NPIs such as *any/either/yet*, licensed only in downward-entailing contexts), and the breakdown of downward entailment under negation ("A man owns a dog" entails "A man owns an animal", but "A man does not own a dog" does not entail "A man does not own an animal").

The field has shifted from treating negation as a standalone *detection* task to probing its effect inside language models, organized along three capability tiers: (1) **sensitivity** to negation via cloze completion of factual statements; (2) **lexical semantics** of negation via antonym/synonym classification; and (3) **reasoning** with negation via natural language inference (NLI). Within this frame, two findings dominate. First, **inverse scaling**: larger LMs become *more* insensitive to negation on cloze tasks — Kassner & Schütze (2020) showed LMs complete negated and affirmative cloze identically, and Jang et al. (2022b) operationalize this with **MKR-NQ** (Masked Knowledge Retrieval – Negated Query), a negated version of LAMA (Petroni et al., 2019); Wei et al. (2022b) and the Inverse Scaling Prize (Neg-QA) frame the trend as inverse scaling. Second, **instruction-tuning as a partial fix**: inverse-scaling curves can flatten or reverse for large instruction-tuned models (FLAN-PaLM-540B), with parallel reports for InstructGPT and FLAN-T5. *Why* tuning helps is asserted empirically, not explained.

The source work flags two limitations that motivate everything below. It is **English-only**, and it carries a **ChatGPT caveat**: a small-scale probe found ChatGPT performing strongly on challenging samples, so the authors concede their conclusions "may not hold for newer LLMs." That caveat is an invitation, but it conceals a deeper measurement risk. Negation is the canonical setting where a model can score well via *lexical shortcuts* — antonym co-occurrence, lexical overlap, and label priors — rather than genuine compositional understanding. NLI already had its "HANS moment", exposing surface-heuristic reliance and annotation artifacts via hypothesis-only baselines (McCoy et al., 2019; Gururangan et al., 2018; Poliak et al., 2018) under the broader shortcut-learning lens (Geirhos et al., 2020). Negation-in-LLMs has not yet had that reckoning. The decade-old "LMs are negation-blind" finding is now being overwritten by an optimistic "instruction-tuning/RLHF fixed it" narrative that is propagating into deployment-readiness reasoning — without the controls that would tell us whether the improvement is real.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

Reading the source introduction against the broader literature surfaces four concrete weaknesses that no single prior paper addresses together:

- **Sensitivity ≠ understanding.** Prior probes measure whether a prediction *changes* when "not" appears, not whether the model composes the negation. A model can be "sensitive" via a crude "negation → flip" heuristic with zero compositional content, and no shortcut baseline is run on the same items to rule this out.
- **The instruction-tuning claim is correlational.** Improvements are inferred by comparing different model families and sizes (e.g., Truong et al., 2023, "Language models are not naysayers"; García-Ferrero et al., 2023, "This is not a Dataset"), where tuning is entangled with data, scale, and architecture. The clean within-model **base-vs-instruct** ablation was logistically impossible until matched open-weight pairs arrived in 2023–2024.
- **Negation is treated as monolithic.** Explicit, implicit, quantifier, gradable, NPI, and downward-entailment negation are different computations, but aggregate scores hide which type is genuinely handled. Harder-tier resources (MoNLI, NaN-NLI, CondaQA, Hossain 2022) have never been organized into one type-stratified axis on matched models with controls.
- **No minimal-pair shortcut controls.** The contrastive minimal-pair paradigm (BLiMP; Warstadt et al., 2020) has never been applied to arbitrary negation types against base/instruct LLMs, partly because auto-building fluent, meaning-controlled affirmative↔negated pairs is non-trivial (naive "not" insertion yields ungrammatical or degenerate items). The field defaulted to static benchmarks and leaderboard accuracy.

**Condensed related-work positioning.**

| Prior line | What it did | What it missed → our move |
|---|---|---|
| Ettinger 2020; Kassner & Schütze 2020 | Showed negation-insensitivity in cloze | Pre-instruct, sensitivity-only → re-ask on matched base-vs-instruct with shortcut controls |
| LAMA / MKR-NQ (Petroni 2019; Jang 2022b) | Negated factual cloze | No label-prior/overlap baseline → our Tier-I with shortcut baselines |
| Truong 2023; García-Ferrero 2023 | Evaluated modern instruct LLMs, noted shallow-cue reliance | Cross-family/correlational, reliance asserted not quantified → within-model causal + quantified shortcut contribution |
| MoNLI; NaN-NLI; CondaQA; Hossain 2022 | Harder implicit/compositional data | Used in isolation → combined into a type-stratified Tier-III with controls |
| HANS; Gururangan/Poliak; Geirhos 2020 | Shortcut/artifact detection in NLI | Never applied to negation-in-LLMs → import directly |
| Warstadt 2019/2020; Truong 2022a/b | Minimal-pair paradigm + gradable/NPI catalogue | Not built for arbitrary negation types vs base/instruct → auto-generate across types, require coherent flips |
| Wei 2022b; Inverse Scaling Prize (Neg-QA) | Some negation worsens with scale | No mechanism → label-prior reliance as candidate mechanism |

**Brainstormed directions (and why the chosen one wins).** Phase-1 brainstorming considered: (i) *mechanistic* analysis of why instruction-tuning helps (high novelty, low feasibility — interpretability is noisy and cannot touch 540B); (ii) a *training-time fix* for negation insensitivity (feasible, but easy to overfit one benchmark); (iii) *re-benchmarking* modern RLHF/reasoning models (very feasible, but risks being "just a leaderboard re-run"); (iv) *implicit/clinical* negation (annotation-heavy, data-access issues); (v) *multilingual* negation (high novelty, but quality non-English benchmarks are sparse); and (vi) a **shortcut-resistant contrastive minimal-pair diagnosis** of whether alignment buys genuine vs. shortcut understanding. Direction (vi) wins because it is the only one that simultaneously is novel (it reframes the open question from "*do* big/tuned models handle negation?" to "*is their apparent competence real or a shortcut?*"), directly and falsifiably resolves the paper's own ChatGPT caveat, and is fully feasible on academic compute using existing public benchmarks plus automatically generated minimal pairs and matched base-vs-instruct open-weight models.

## 3. Novel Insights and Recommendations

**The locked idea.** We will determine whether the negation competence that instruction-tuning/RLHF appears to grant modern LLMs reflects *genuine compositional understanding* or *lexical shortcut behavior*, using contrastive affirmative-versus-negated **minimal pairs** stratified by negation type (explicit / implicit / compositional) and quantified against explicit shortcut baselines on matched base-vs-instruct models.

**Borrowed-Rigor Novelty.** The durable contribution is methodological: we import the contrastive minimal-pair and shortcut-control reporting standards that gave NLI its HANS/BLiMP moment (McCoy 2019; Warstadt 2020; Geirhos 2020) into negation-in-LLMs for the first time, at scale and across negation types. The verdict on today's specific models is perishable; the *measurement protocol* — and the discipline of asking "is this number real or a shortcut?" — is the lasting result.

**Deployment-risk-map framing.** Polarity flips are catastrophic precisely where instruct models are being deployed on the "negation is fixed" story: clinical ("no evidence of malignancy"), legal, pharmacovigilance, and safety ("not to receive anticoagulants"). The output is therefore not just a score but a *deployment-risk map* — which negation constructions are genuinely handled and which still require guardrails, abstention, or human review.

**The NSG instrument.** The headline instrument is the **Negation Sensitivity Gap (NSG)**: the correctly-signed difference between a model's behavior on the affirmative member and the negated member of a minimal pair, measured as both Δaccuracy and Δlog-likelihood / mean signed ΔP(gold). Genuine competence yields a large, correctly-signed NSG; shortcut behavior yields NSG ≈ 0 even when headline accuracy is high.

**Recommendations for the field.** Negation results on LLMs should be reported with (1) within-model base-vs-instruct ablations rather than cross-family comparisons; (2) per-negation-type breakdowns instead of aggregate accuracy; (3) contrastive minimal pairs with explicit shortcut baselines (lexical-overlap, label-prior, hypothesis-only) so the shortcut-free residual is visible; and (4) dual scoring (log-likelihood and generative) with format controls so apparent gains are not mistaken for pure format-following.

## 4. The 5Q Research Proposal

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

To what extent does the apparent negation competence of instruction-tuned/RLHF LLMs reflect genuine compositional negation understanding rather than lexical shortcut heuristics — as revealed by contrastive affirmative-versus-negated minimal-pair evaluation stratified by negation type (explicit, implicit, and compositional)?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Negation is the canonical stress test that separates pattern-matching from understanding: it inverts truth conditions while leaving surface form almost intact, so a co-occurrence-tracking model is exposed precisely here. The decade-old "LMs are negation-blind" finding (Ettinger 2020; Kassner & Schütze 2020) is now being overwritten by an optimistic "instruction-tuning/RLHF fixed it" narrative that is propagating into deployment-readiness reasoning. If that narrative is partly a shortcut-learning artifact, the community is recalibrating its trust on a false signal. Methodologically, negation is the sharpest available probe for shortcut learning (Geirhos 2020): NLI already had its "HANS moment" via hypothesis-only baselines and artifact analyses (McCoy 2019; Gururangan 2018; Poliak 2018), but negation-in-LLMs has not — this work imports contrastive minimal-pair and shortcut-control reporting and establishes it as a standard. That standard is the durable contribution; the verdict on today's models is perishable. The future-research impact is threefold: (1) it disciplines the instruction-tuning literature with a causal, matched base-vs-instruct within-model ablation instead of cross-family correlation; (2) it relocates the frontier by showing *where* competence is real (explicit negation) versus illusory (implicit and compositional — quantifiers, gradable adjectives, NPIs, downward entailment); and (3) it connects to scaling science (inverse-scaling Neg-QA) by offering label-prior reliance as a candidate mechanism. The stakes are practical and asymmetric: polarity flips are catastrophic in clinical, legal, pharmacovigilance, and safety settings ("no evidence of malignancy", "not to receive anticoagulants") — exactly where instruct models are being deployed on the "negation is fixed" story. The concrete deliverable is a deployment-risk map of which negation constructions still need guardrails or abstention.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The naive instinct — "re-run benchmarks on instruct models, read off accuracy, higher = competence" — fails, and *why* it fails is itself the contribution. (a) **Building valid minimal pairs is hard.** Affirmative↔negated pairs must flip the gold label deterministically while preserving fluency, holding content/length/syntax constant, and adding no new lexical cue. Explicit "not" insertion is tractable but risky (an NLI label can move entailment→contradiction *or* neutral; double negation and scope corrupt the gold). Implicit negation ("failed to arrive" = didn't arrive; "denied") has no surface cue, so lexical substitution reintroduces a content-word confound. Compositional cases (all/no/not all/few; gradable ¬happy ≠ sad; NPIs *any/ever* licensed only in downward-entailing contexts; downward entailment) flip the entailment direction of the *whole sentence*, so the gold depends on a reasoning chain rather than a token, auto-verifying flips is non-trivial, and a "not"-insertion template simply breaks on "few". Invalid pairs masquerade as model errors. (b) **Disentangling understanding from shortcuts needs causal controls.** Accuracy is non-identifying: a high score is consistent with competence *or* shortcut. Negation datasets are confounded by label priors (negated NLI skews "contradiction"), lexical-overlap heuristics, and partial-input artifacts (Poliak/Gururangan). Only the *gap* between the full model and shortcut baselines, plus the *drop* under shortcut-feature ablation, licenses a causal claim. (c) **Extraction confounds the model axis.** Base and instruct models cannot be probed identically — chat models hedge, refuse, or wrap answers — so a brittle regex extractor adds error correlated with model type, and CoT can leak the answer or change the task; if extraction quality differs across the axis you study, the "effect" may be a parsing artifact. (d) **Scoring is double-edged.** Log-likelihood is clean and prior-controllable and works on base models, but it is sensitive to surface-form/length priors and to negation-cue tokenization (not / n't / cannot / no differ); generative scoring matches deployment but needs extraction. Running a single method mismeasures, so we run both and treat divergence as signal. (e) **There is no oracle for "genuine understanding,"** so it must be operationalized via convergent measures — negation-sensitivity gap, paraphrase/template consistency, pair-consistency (correct only if *both* members are correct), and distractor/shortcut-ablation robustness — and a competence claim must survive all of them. (f) **Base-vs-instruct is confounded by format-following:** instruct models do-the-task-as-asked better, which is real but is *not* negation competence, and it inflates instruct accuracy; this is the most likely way the field over-credited RLHF for negation, so format must be held constant (matched prompts, identical extraction/scoring, and a format-only control task). Bottom line: accuracy is non-identifying, benchmarks are artifact-laden, extraction and scoring are confounded with the model axis, valid minimal pairs are hard, and understanding has no oracle — so any reported "negation gain" is uninterpretable without contrastive minimal pairs + shortcut baselines + dual scoring + format controls.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Every prior line supplies one ingredient and misses the others; nobody combined within-model causal design + type stratification + minimal-pair contrast + shortcut-control baselines. **Gap 1 — sensitivity, not understanding:** prior probes (Ettinger; Kassner & Schütze; LAMA/MKR-NQ, Petroni 2019/Jang 2022b) measure whether a prediction changes when "not" appears, not whether the model composes the negation, and run no shortcut baseline on the same items — a model can be "sensitive" via a negation→flip heuristic with zero composition. **Gap 2 — the instruction-tuning claim is correlational:** modern evaluations (Truong 2023, "Language models are not naysayers"; García-Ferrero 2023, "This is not a Dataset") compare across families and sizes where tuning is entangled with data, scale, and architecture; the clean same-model base-vs-instruct ablation was logistically impossible until matched open-weight pairs arrived in 2023–2024 (Llama base+chat, Mistral, OLMo base+SFT+DPO, Qwen). **Gap 3 — negation treated as monolithic:** explicit/implicit/quantifier/gradable/NPI/downward-entailment are different computations (Truong 2022a/b; Warstadt 2019/2020; MoNLI, Geiger 2020), but aggregate scores hide which is real, and the harder-tier resources (MoNLI, NaN-NLI, CondaQA, Hossain 2022) were never organized into one type-stratified axis on matched models with controls. **Gap 4 — no minimal-pair contrast for negation:** the minimal-pair paradigm (BLiMP; Warstadt 2020) was never applied to arbitrary negation types against base/instruct LLMs, because auto-building fluent, meaning-controlled affirmative↔negated pairs across quantifier/gradable/NPI cases is non-trivial (naive "not" insertion → ungrammatical or degenerate), so the field defaulted to static benchmarks and leaderboard accuracy. The underlying barrier is incentives: the field optimized for leaderboard accuracy, and asking "is the number real or a shortcut?" requires controls that only *lower* your headline — so there was no incentive until clinical/legal/safety stakes and inverse-scaling surprises made it unavoidable. How we differ, on identical items and models: (1) within-model causal base-vs-instruct plus a small API set for external validity; (2) a three-tier, type-stratified axis (MKR-NQ → antonym/synonym → MoNLI/NaN-NLI/CondaQA/Hossain); (3) auto-built affirmative↔negated minimal pairs (the BLiMP/HANS paradigm imported to negation at scale, fluency- and meaning-controlled); and (4) explicit lexical-overlap + label-prior shortcut baselines that quantify how much apparent competence is semantics-free. The result is a decomposition no prior paper can produce: per negation type, how much of the instruct advantage survives after subtracting what a shortcut would have gotten.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**Models (scaling axis).** Matched base↔instruct open-weight pairs: Llama-3-8B base↔Instruct and Llama-3-70B base↔Instruct (for capacity); Mistral-7B-v0.3 base↔Instruct; OLMo-7B base↔SFT↔Instruct/DPO (to separate SFT from preference-tuning); Qwen2.5-7B base↔Instruct; plus Pythia 160M→12B for a clean scaling axis. A small paid-API RLHF/reasoning set (a GPT-4-class model, a Claude, and an o-series-style reasoning model), generative-only, covers the modern-frontier caveat.

**Data — three tiers, all augmented with minimal pairs and stratified by negation type.** *Tier I — Sensitivity:* negated-LAMA/MKR-NQ cloze (does the predicted distribution move when negation is inserted?). *Tier II — Lexical semantics:* antonym vs. synonym classification from WordNet-derived sets plus gradable triples (happy / unhappy / not happy / sad) to test contrary vs. contradictory (¬happy ≠ sad). *Tier III — Reasoning:* MoNLI (downward entailment), NaN-NLI, CondaQA, and negation subsets of SNLI/MNLI/RTE (Hossain 2022).

**Auto-constructed minimal pairs (novel data layer).** Templated cue insertion/removal (explicit); verified antonym/converse substitution (implicit: arrive↔fail-to-arrive, remember↔forget, allow↔deny); quantifier/scope edits (compositional: all↔not-all↔no↔few, adding/removing NPIs in downward-entailing contexts). A **validation gate** per pair combines an NLI-model + rule-based label-flip check, a fluency/grammar filter (perplexity + grammar), content-word/length matching, and a human spot-check, targeting ≥95% gold validity per stratum. Each item carries a negation-type label (explicit / implicit / compositional-{quantifier, gradable, NPI, downward-entailment}).

**Shortcut baselines (identification layer, same items, subtractable).** Lexical-overlap; label-prior/majority; hypothesis-only/partial-input (Poliak/Gururangan); bag-of-words TF-IDF logistic; a content-word-matched control (above-baseline performance here is the cleanest evidence of real processing); and a negation-blind upper-bound check (strip/garble the cue — a competent model drops, a shortcut model does not).

**Metrics.** **Negation Sensitivity Gap (NSG)** — the headline novel metric — is the Δ between the affirmative and the correctly-flipped negated member, reported as both Δaccuracy and Δlog-likelihood / mean signed ΔP(gold); genuine competence ⇒ a large, correctly-signed NSG, shortcut ⇒ NSG ≈ 0. We also report pair-consistency/contrast-set accuracy (correct only if both members are correct), a shortcut-reliance score (the drop when shortcut features are ablated), calibration (ECE), prompt-robustness (variance across templates), and per-negation-type breakdowns, base→instruct deltas, and scaling curves.

**Conditions.** Zero-shot, few-shot (k = 2/5), and CoT (does CoT reverse inverse-scaling/negation-blindness?); log-likelihood *and* generative scoring (both reported); and format controls (format-matched prompts plus a negation-free format-only control task to subtract pure format-following).

**Ablations.** Remove the minimal-pair control (showing naive accuracy overstates competence vs. pair-consistency); per-tier and per-type; base-vs-instruct holding format constant; SFT-only vs. DPO (OLMo).

**Expected outcomes (falsifiable).** (1) Headline accuracy rises with instruction-tuning but NSG does not move much — near-zero NSG on compositional/implicit negation, surviving mainly on explicit. (2) Shortcut-reliance explains a substantial share of the gain (accuracy collapses under hypothesis-only/overlap/prior ablation; shortcut-reliance correlates with gain size across pairs). (3) CoT helps the reasoning tier and partially reverses inverse scaling there but does not repair Tier-I sensitivity or Tier-II gradable contrasts. (4) Inverse-scaling/negation-blindness persists on Pythia and the sensitivity tier even at 70B. (5) Frontier API models partially survive on explicit negation but degrade on compositional minimal pairs and pair-consistency (the modern caveat is real but not total). (6) Most of the instruct bump is format-following + explicit-lexical handling, not new compositional competence (SFT → format, DPO → little extra sensitivity). **Falsifier:** if instruct models retain a full correctly-signed NSG with near-zero shortcut-reliance across all tiers and types *and* the advantage persists after holding format constant and subtracting shortcut baselines — and if pair-consistency on compositional/implicit negation tracks headline accuracy — then the shortcut account is wrong.

**Feasibility.** Inference is the bulk of the work: 7–8B models run on one A100-80GB, 70B on 8×A100 (tensor-parallel or 4-bit), and Pythia is cheap; the full battery is on the order of a few thousand GPU-hours (1–2 weeks on a shared 8×A100 node). Optional cheap LoRA probes support the format-match test. The API budget is a few hundred dollars on a stratified 2–5k subset × 3 models × {zero-shot, CoT}. Data construction is CPU + small-NLI validation + a bounded human spot-check. **Risks/mitigations:** invalid pairs → multi-filter gate + spot-check + drop low-validity strata; extraction confound → dual scoring + model-agnostic extractor + audit + reported failure rates; prompt-sensitivity → multi-template + a robustness metric; surface-form/length priors → length normalization + PMI correction + generative cross-check; format confound → format-only control + LoRA format-match probe; contamination → lean on the novel auto-built pairs for primary claims; API nondeterminism → pinned versions, temperature 0, seeds, reported variance; multiple comparisons → pre-register the primary hypotheses (NSG on compositional negation; shortcut-reliance vs. gain correlation), correct for multiplicity, and report effect sizes with confidence intervals.

## 5. References and Evidence

All references are real; García-Ferrero et al. (2023) and Truong et al. (2023) were web-verified.

- Ettinger, A. (2020). *What BERT is not: Lessons from a new suite of psycholinguistic diagnostics for language models.* TACL.
- Kassner, N., & Schütze, H. (2020). *Negated and Misprimed Probes for Pretrained Language Models: Birds Can Talk, But Cannot Fly.* ACL.
- Petroni, F., et al. (2019). *Language Models as Knowledge Bases? (LAMA).* EMNLP.
- Jang, M., et al. (2022b). *MKR-NQ: Masked Knowledge Retrieval – Negated Query (negated LAMA).*
- Hossain, M. M., et al. (2022). *An Analysis of Negation in Natural Language Understanding Corpora.* ACL.
- Hossain, M. M., & Blanco, E. (2022). *Negation in downstream tasks.*
- Truong, T. H., et al. (2022a/b). *Negation surveys; gradable cases.*
- Truong, T. H., et al. (2022). *NaN-NLI: A Naturalistic Negation NLI dataset.*
- Truong, T. H., et al. (2023). *Language Models Are Not Naysayers: An Analysis of Language Models on Negation Benchmarks.* *SEM (web-verified).
- García-Ferrero, I., et al. (2023). *This is not a Dataset: A Large Negation Benchmark to Challenge Large Language Models.* EMNLP (web-verified).
- Geiger, A., et al. (2020). *MoNLI: Monotonicity NLI.*
- Ravichander, A., et al. (2022). *CondaQA: A Contrastive Reading Comprehension Dataset for Reasoning about Negation.* EMNLP Findings.
- McCoy, R. T., et al. (2019). *Right for the Wrong Reasons: Diagnosing Syntactic Heuristics in NLI (HANS).* ACL.
- Gururangan, S., et al. (2018). *Annotation Artifacts in Natural Language Inference Data.* NAACL.
- Poliak, A., et al. (2018). *Hypothesis Only Baselines in Natural Language Inference.* *SEM.
- Geirhos, R., et al. (2020). *Shortcut Learning in Deep Neural Networks.* Nature Machine Intelligence.
- Warstadt, A., et al. (2019). *Investigating BERT's Knowledge of Language: NPI Licensing.*
- Warstadt, A., et al. (2020). *BLiMP: The Benchmark of Linguistic Minimal Pairs for English.* TACL.
- Wei, J., et al. (2022b). *Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models / inverse scaling.*
- Inverse Scaling Prize (2022–2023). *Neg-QA task.*
